edges.quote: Against the Shivers | was helpless,” he confessed to Septon Barth. “Against the Vulture, | was a king again.”
edges.note: Jaehaerys frames his zealous prosecution of Lord Rogar's War/the Vulture hunt as psychological compensation for his helplessness during the Shivers.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES not CAUSES: the war's origin was Rogar Baratheon's request and the Vulture King's raiding, not the plague. The plague supplies the king's personal drive to embrace the war, a disposition-level motivation, not a forcing cause.

edges.quote: A day and a half after she had woken her mother from sleep complaining of feeling cold, the little princess was dead.
edges.note: Six-year-old Princess Daenerys dies of the Shivers within two days of falling ill.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: direct, unmediated disease death, a specific instance of the general epidemic already established by the plague node; no agency-collapse concern.

edges.quote: been attending council since the death of Princess Daenerys.
edges.note: Alysanne stops attending council after Daenerys's death.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES targeting the character's changed behavior/disposition, the first documented instance of her withdrawing from governance after losing a child.

edges.quote: Amidst the chaos, His Grace would lose another of his lords, not to the Shivers but to ignorance and hate.
edges.note: The plague's collapse of the City Watch (commander Qarl Corbray dead of Shivers) creates the lawless conditions in which a hungry mob murders Rego Draz.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the epidemic is an explicit precondition (dead Watch commander, lawlessness, fear of foreigners blamed for the plague) but the mob's free choice to attack produced the murder; not a forced CAUSES.

edges.quote: Under torture, he soon gave up the names of the other attackers, and they were taken one and all.
edges.note: The king's manhunt after Rego Draz's murder directly produces the killers' capture and execution.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: real causation through the intermediate manhunt/interrogation steps, all directly narrated as one continuous royal response.

edges.quote: Vaegon is no maester...but he could well have the makings of an archmaester in him. The Citadel is the greatest repository of knowledge in the known world. Send him there.
edges.note: Grand Maester Elysar's counsel directly persuades Jaehaerys to send Vaegon to the Citadel instead of continuing the search for a bride.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Elysar's suggestion is the proximate driver of the king's decision ("his words struck home," three days later Vaegon is summoned and told he ships for Oldtown).

edges.quote: Plainly, the king and queen concluded afterward, some other bride must needs be found for Vaegon.
edges.note: Vaegon's public rejection of Daella ends the presumed sibling match and starts the years-long, ultimately failed, search for a bride that ends with Elysar's Citadel proposal.
edges.rationale: CAUSES through intermediate steps: the text explicitly narrates this as one continuous thread ("we shall return to Prince Vaegon...in due course", then resolves it years later at the Citadel decision).

edges.quote: “I would never marry her,” the boy said, in front of half the court.
edges.note: Vaegon is the agent of the public insult.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the new node's central actor.

edges.quote: Her sixteenth nameday was fast approaching, and with it her womanhood. Queen Alysanne was at her wit's end, and the king had lost his patience.
edges.note: The failed Blackwood match leaves Daella still unwed as her womanhood approaches, feeding the king's impatience that produces the year-end marriage ultimatum whose consequence (marriage, then pregnancy) kills her.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the collapse is a precondition for the mounting urgency, but Jaehaerys's forced-marriage ultimatum is the mediating free decision; preserves agency per the ladder rather than false CAUSES straight to her death.

edges.quote: It all fell to pieces when Daella learned that the Blackwoods kept the old gods,
edges.note: Daella's own refusal ends the courtship. [S204: quote trimmed at OCR blank-line break; continuation 'say her vows before a weirwood' is 2 lines down in the file]
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the new node's central actor.

edges.quote: Royce Blackwood was a gifted bowman, a fine swordsman, and a singer, who melted Daella’s heart with ballads of his own composition.
edges.note: Royce Blackwood was the suitor whose courtship collapsed.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the new node's second party.

edges.quote: first hint of the rift that would open between her and her king.
edges.note: Gyldayn identifies Daella's death as the first hint of the estrangement between Jaehaerys and Alysanne later called the First Quarrel / Great Rift.
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: the text calls it only the "first hint" — one contributing wound (alongside Alyssa's and Viserra's deaths and the Saera quarrel narrated later in the section) among several that together produce the eventual open rift; preserves the compound, multi-cause nature of the estrangement.

edges.quote: We ought never have pushed her into marriage.
edges.note: Alysanne directly blames Jaehaerys for insisting Daella marry — the seed of her later resentment.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the death drives Alysanne's changed disposition/blame toward the king, a character-target form distinct from E14-E12's event-target form.

edges.quote: missed Lord Cameron...and struck Prince Aemon, standing at his side.
edges.note: A stray Myrish crossbow bolt aimed at the Evenstar of Tarth kills Prince Aemon instead.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring Aemon as victim of the established myrish-bloodbath hub node (no dedicated death-of-Aemon node exists; myrish-bloodbath already carries the full narrative).

edges.quote: Baelon the Brave played his part in the slaughter, with Dark Sister in his hand.
edges.note: Baelon, enraged by his brother's death, joins the retaliatory slaughter of the Myrish invaders on Vhagar.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring Baelon's combatant role in the same hub event.

edges.quote: the Old King never dreamed that Aemon’'s death in 92 AC would be like the hellhorns of Valyrian legend, bringing death and destruction down on all those who heard their sound.
edges.note: Gyldayn's own foreshadowing statement frames Aemon's death as the root of the eventual succession crisis that produces the Great Council of 101 AC and, later, the Dance's succession grievance. [S204 adjudication: CAUSES->ENABLES — Gyldayn hellhorns line foreshadows, does not narrate the mechanical chain]
edges.rationale: CAUSES per Gyldayn's explicit (if metaphorical) framing, but downgraded to tier-2/disputed since this section does not itself narrate the intervening mechanics (Baelon becoming heir, Baelon's own later death) — those likely live in section 15. Flagged for cross-check there rather than asserted as a mechanically verified chain.

edges.quote: the queen gave birth to her tenth child and sixth daughter, the beautiful Viserra.
edges.note: Stub wiring: mother.
edges.rationale: Baseline AGENT_IN mother edge per section instructions.

edges.quote: the queen gave birth to her tenth child and sixth daughter, the beautiful Viserra.
edges.note: Stub wiring: the child.
edges.rationale: Matches the existing PARTICIPATES_IN(child)/AGENT_IN(mother) convention used elsewhere for FAB birth events in this graph.

edges.quote: Princess Rhaenys was born on the seventh day of the seventh moon of the year, which the septons judged to be highly auspicious.
edges.note: Stub wiring: mother (Jocelyn, Aemon's wife).
edges.rationale: Baseline AGENT_IN mother edge per section instructions.

edges.quote: Princess Rhaenys was born on the seventh day of the seventh moon of the year, which the septons judged to be highly auspicious.
edges.note: Stub wiring: the child.
edges.rationale: Matches existing sibling-edge convention.

edges.quote: Princess Gael was born in 80 AC, when the queen was forty-four.
edges.note: Stub wiring: mother.
edges.rationale: Baseline AGENT_IN mother edge per section instructions.

edges.quote: Princess Gael was born in 80 AC, when the queen was forty-four.
edges.note: Stub wiring: the child.
edges.rationale: Matches existing sibling-edge convention.

edges.quote: Prince Valerion was born in 77 AC, after another troubled labor that saw Alysanne confined to her bed for half a year.
edges.note: Stub wiring: mother.
edges.rationale: Baseline AGENT_IN mother edge per section instructions.

edges.quote: Prince Valerion was born in 77 AC, after another troubled labor that saw Alysanne confined to her bed for half a year.
edges.note: Stub wiring: the child.
edges.rationale: Matches existing sibling-edge convention.

edges.quote: Like his brother Gaemon four years earlier, he was a small and sickly babe, and never thrived.
edges.note: Valerion's difficult birth and lifelong frailty directly precede his death before his first nameday.
edges.rationale: CAUSES: the text explicitly attributes his death to frailty stemming from the troubled birth ("never thrived... In 78 AC, Valerion died"); the exact case the section notes call out ("a birth's complications causing a death"), no mediating free decision.

edges.quote: year later came another daughter, Daella.
edges.note: Stub wiring: mother. [S204: leading 'A' of the sentence sits on the previous wrapped OCR line; quote is verbatim from file line 19]
edges.rationale: Baseline AGENT_IN mother edge per section instructions.

edges.quote: year later came another daughter, Daella.
edges.note: Stub wiring: the child.
edges.rationale: Matches existing sibling-edge convention.

edges.quote: In 93 AC, Prince Baelon’s sixteen-year-old son, Viserys, entered the Dragonpit and claimed Balerion.
edges.note: Stub wiring: Viserys is the agent claiming the dragon.
edges.rationale: No further causal claim is textually supported — Balerion's death less than a year later is presented as the old dragon's natural end, not a consequence of being ridden; role edge only, per section instructions ("causal only where text asserts it").

edges.quote: Only then did the king and queen send for Princess Saera.
edges.note: The overnight investigation triggered by the Blue Pearl discovery (interrogating Perianne, Alys, squires, and grooms) leads directly to Saera's own confrontation before the Iron Throne.
edges.rationale: CAUSES through the intermediate same-night interrogation the text narrates as one unbroken sequence; roster-flagged causally-dark node with a clean, explicit textual link.

edges.quote: A fortnight later, Jaehaerys and Alysanne gave another of their daughters over to the Faith. Princess Saera, who was not quite seventeen, departed King’s Landing for Oldtown, where her sister Septa Maegelle was to
edges.note: Saera's sentence at her judgment (confinement, then the Faith at Oldtown) places her at the motherhouse from which she later flees in 85 AC.
edges.rationale: CAUSES through intermediate steps (a year and a half of confinement at the motherhouse) explicitly narrated as the direct consequence of her judgment; roster-flagged causally-dark node.

new_nodes.name: Death of Princess Daenerys
new_nodes.identity: Six-year-old Princess Daenerys, daughter of Jaehaerys I and Alysanne, dies of the Shivers a day and a half after waking her mother complaining of cold — the epidemic's most personally devastating loss for the royal couple, and the death that shatters their belief in the Doctrine of Exceptionalism.

new_nodes.name: Vaegon's insult of Daella in court
new_nodes.identity: Prince Vaegon publicly declares before half the court that he would never marry his sister Daella, mocking her illiteracy; the humiliation ends the presumed Vaegon-Daella match and forces the king and queen to seek him a different bride.

new_nodes.name: Collapse of Daella's courtship with Royce Blackwood
new_nodes.identity: A near-betrothal between Princess Daella and Royce Blackwood of Raventree falls apart when Daella learns the Blackwoods keep the old gods and she would have to say her wedding vows before a weirwood, which she refuses in horror ("I'd go to hell").

new_nodes.name: Death of Prince Valerion
new_nodes.identity: Prince Valerion, Jaehaerys and Alysanne's small and sickly son, dies a fortnight short of his first nameday, having never thrived since his difficult birth the year before.

flags: DUPLICATE NODE: lord-rogars-war and third-dornish-war appear to be the same event under two names (Gyldayn explicitly says the 'Third Dornish War' label is a misnomer and the smallfolk name, Lord Rogar's War, 'is far more apt'). third-dornish-war has the richer wiki-sourced content and existing edges, so E14-E01 targets it; lord-rogars-war (already S203-touched with one quote) has zero edges. Recommend a merge/SAME_AS pass rather than parallel wiring of both.

GAP NOTED, NOT WIRED: Prince Gaemon (born 73 AC, died just short of the new year, not quite three moons old — fab-the-long-reign-cont-14-p02:65) is the same birth-complication-causes-death pattern as Valerion, but Gaemon was not in this packet's stubs array and no birth-of-prince-gaemon/death-of-prince-gaemon event nodes exist yet. Flagging for a future pass rather than minting outside scope.

E14-E16 (myrish-bloodbath CAUSES great-council-of-101-ac) rests on Gyldayn's own foreshadowing line in this section; the mechanical chain (Baelon becoming heir over Rhaenys, then Baelon's own death) is not narrated in fab-the-long-reign-cont-14 and likely belongs to section 15 (cont-15) or the Great Council's own section. Cross-check there — do not treat this edge as a substitute for the direct mechanical chain once that section is wired.

No death-of-prince-aemon-targaryen-son-of-jaehaerys-i node exists (or was needed): the established myrish-bloodbath hub node already carries the full narrative of his death, so I wired role edges (VICTIM_IN, FIGHTS_IN) into it rather than minting a duplicate death node.

harvest.note: Kitten-cat economy during the Shivers: "suddenly every house in the Seven Kingdoms...required a cat...kittens were selling for as much as destriers" — nice smallfolk-economics color, not currently used anywhere.

Homeless quote, food/hospitality-adjacent: the Flea Bottom girl who led the king to Rego Draz's killers is taken in by Alysanne, scrubbed, fed "hot bread and bacon," and asks to work in the kitchens — "That's where they keeps the bread." Good hospitality/food-as-kindness texture, unattached to any node.

Septon Barth's description of infant Viserys: "a plump and pleasant lad, who laughed more than any babe I've ever known, and nursed so lustily he drank his wet nurse dry" — vivid physical-description quote, homeless (birth-of-viserys-i-targaryen node not touched this pass).

Princess Gael's true cause of death (seduced by a traveling singer, stillborn son, then drowned herself) is only revealed "after both her parents were gone" — explicit narrator's-delayed-reveal structure worth flagging for a foreshadowing/discovery-structure pass if one is ever run over FAB.

Alysanne's summary tally of her thirteen children's fates ("Aegon, Gaemon, and Valerion died as babes. The Shivers took Daenerys...A crossbow slew Prince Aemon. Alyssa and Daella died in childbed, Viserra drunk in the street.") is a dense one-line index of nearly every death node in this section — good candidate quote for a future summary/overview node on Jaehaerys and Alysanne's children if one gets built.
